perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as the Receiving Party uses to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'the same degree of care, but no less than', the modified clause lowers the standard of care required to protect Confidential Information. This weakens the protection for Confidential Information and may conflict with section 6, which asserts that remedies at law may be inadequate due to the unique nature of the information requiring greater care.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Because of the unique and proprietary nature of Confidential Information, it is understood and agreed that remedies at law for a breach by the Receiving Party of its obligations under this Agreement may be inadequate and that in the event of such breach or threatened breach the 1 Disclosing Party will be entitled to seek equitable relief, including without limitation injunctive relief and specific performance. The remedies set forth in this Section are in addition to any other remedies available for any such breach or threatened breach, including the recovery of damages from the breaching party.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Receiving Party to use 'the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care' to protect Confidential Information. The changed text lowers this standard to 'a reasonable degree of care', removing the requirement to provide the 'same degree of care' as they do to their own confidential information. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that legal remedies may be inadequate because of the 'unique' nature of Confidential Information and the Disclosing Party will seek equitable relief requiring greater care. The change in standard from 'same degree of care' to 'reasonable degree of care' weakens the protection and thus the remedy requirement stated in the contradicted text, which requires more than reasonable care for remedy.

perturbation.original_text: Except as permitted by the previous paragraph, neither the Receiving Party nor the Receiving Party’s representatives will disclose to any person the fact that the Confidential information has been made available to the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party’s representatives or that the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party’s representatives have inspected any portion of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither the Receiving Party nor the Receiving Party’s representatives will disclose to any person that the Confidential information has been made available to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'the Receiving Party’s representatives or that the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party’s representatives have inspected any portion of the Confidential Information', the modified text creates uncertainty about whether the Receiving Party’s representatives are also bound by the non-disclosure of inspection information. This is inconsistent with section 3, which allows disclosure to representatives only if they are bound by the terms of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party will disclose Confidential Information only to its employees and to its representative whose participation is necessary or helpful to evaluate and advance to the Business Transaction and who have agreed to be bound by the terms of this Agreement. The Receiving Party will limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as the Receiving Party uses to protect its own confidential information. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Agreement, the Receiving Party is not prohibited from disclosing Confidential Information it is legally compelled to disclose (by deposition, subpoena, civil investigative demand, court order or otherwise), provided that the Receiving Party gives the Disclosing Party prompt written notice of any such required disclosure so that the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy. If such protective order or other remedy is not obtained and the Disclosing Party has not waived compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will furnish only that portion of the Confidential Information which the Receiving Party is advised by opinion of counsel it is legally required to furnish and will exercise reasonable efforts to limit the scope and content of such disclosure and obtain confidential treatment of such Confidential Information, Furthermore, the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement do not apply with respect to any Confidential Information that (a) is or becomes generally available to the public other than from the improper action of the Receiving Party, (b) is developed by the Receiving Party without reference to the Confidential Information, (c) was in the Receiving Party’s possession before receipt from the Disclosing Party, or (d) is received by the Receiving Party from a third party who is not subject to an obligation of confidentiality owed to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prevented the Receiving Party and its representatives from disclosing that the confidential information was made available or that they inspected it. The changed text removes the restriction on the representatives regarding inspection, creating an inconsistency with the contradicted section, which states that representatives must be bound by the terms of the agreement. The modified text now leaves open the possibility that representatives are not bound by the non-disclosure regarding inspection, contradicting the requirement in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all documents or other materials furnished by the Disclosing Party constituting Confidential Information, together with all copies thereof in the possession, custody or control of the Receiving Party, without retaining any copies. Any destruction must be confirmed by the Receiving Party to the Disclosing Party in writing. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth herein, the Receiving Party is not required to return or destroy computer files stored in the ordinary course of its business as a result of automated back-up procedures (subject, however, to the confidentiality restrictions set forth herein).
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party all documents or other materials furnished by the Disclosing Party constituting Confidential Information. Any destruction must be confirmed by the Receiving Party to the Disclosing Party in writing.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'or destroy all documents... without retaining any copies. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth herein, the Receiving Party is not required to return or destroy computer files stored in the ordinary course of its business as a result of automated back-up procedures (subject, however, to the confidentiality restrictions set forth herein)', the modified clause creates uncertainty about the Receiving Party's obligation to destroy copies and handle backup files containing confidential information, potentially contradicting the overall intent of the agreement to maintain confidentiality. It now becomes ambiguous if the Receiving party can retain copies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information Defined . The term “Confidential Information” means all confidential and proprietary information belonging to one party (the “Disclosing Party”) that is revealed to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) during the Disclosure Period, which begins on the effective date and ends April 26, 2011. Confidential Information, regardless of the form in which it appears, may include, without limitation, information about customers, business and marketing plans, financial data, expertise of employees, forecasts and strategies that is related to the business of the Disclosing Party and is of the type of information that would reasonably be deemed to be confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for the destruction of confidential information with written confirmation, but explicitly stated that backup files were exempt from the return or destruction requirement, subject to confidentiality restrictions. The changed text omits the allowance for destruction, the process for destruction confirmation, and the exception for backup files. This omission creates a contradiction because the contradicted text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly, but the changed text provides no guidance on handling it, specifically concerning backups, potentially conflicting with the Receiving Party's obligation to protect such information under the confidentiality restrictions. Therefore there is now no explicit instruction on whether or not copies can be retained.
